rules.name: no-direct-test-commands-on-host
rules.evidence: Never run pytest, python, or airflow commands directly on the host — always use `breeze`.

rules.name: dag-title-case-prose
rules.evidence: Write **Dag** (title case) in all prose.

rules.name: dag-title-case-prose
rules.evidence: Write **Dag** (title case) in all prose.

rules.name: no-spell-out-dag-acronym
rules.evidence: Don't spell out **Directed Acyclic Graph** except for historical context.

rules.name: no-spell-out-dag-acronym
rules.evidence: Don't spell out **Directed Acyclic Graph** except for historical context.

rules.name: session-no-commit-in-function
rules.evidence: In `airflow-core`, functions with a `session` parameter must not call `session.commit()`.

rules.name: session-no-commit-in-function
rules.evidence: In `airflow-core`, functions with a `session` parameter must not call `session.commit()`.

rules.name: no-new-raise-airflow-exception
rules.evidence: Never add new direct `raise AirflowException(...)` usages — the community is actively reducing them, not adding more, and the `check-no-new-airflow-exceptions` prek hook enforces this across `airflow-core`, `airflow-ctl`, `task-sdk`, `providers`, and `shared`.

rules.name: no-new-raise-airflow-exception
rules.evidence: Never add new direct `raise AirflowException(...)` usages — the community is actively reducing them, not adding more, and the `check-no-new-airflow-exceptions` prek hook enforces this across `airflow-core`, `airflow-ctl`, `task-sdk`, `providers`, and `shared`.

rules.name: no-assert-in-production-code
rules.evidence: No `assert` in production code.

rules.name: no-assert-in-production-code
rules.evidence: No `assert` in production code.

rules.name: duration-use-monotonic-not-time
rules.evidence: `time.monotonic()` for durations, not `time.time()`.

rules.name: duration-use-monotonic-not-time
rules.evidence: `time.monotonic()` for durations, not `time.time()`.

rules.name: core-api-http-exception-boundary
rules.evidence: Translate domain-layer exceptions to `HTTPException` at FastAPI route boundaries... Otherwise they propagate as `500 Internal Server Error`, leaking internals and misleading clients.

rules.name: core-api-http-exception-boundary
rules.evidence: Translate domain-layer exceptions to `HTTPException` at FastAPI route boundaries... Otherwise they propagate as `500 Internal Server Error`, leaking internals and misleading clients.

rules.name: bulk-write-batching-in-scheduler
rules.evidence: Bulk `DELETE`/`UPDATE` in the scheduler loop or any synchronous interval task... must be batched with `LIMIT` and committed between batches — never issue a single unbounded bulk write against a user-driven table.

rules.name: bulk-write-batching-in-scheduler
rules.evidence: Bulk `DELETE`/`UPDATE` in the scheduler loop or any synchronous interval task... must be batched with `LIMIT` and committed between batches — never issue a single unbounded bulk write against a user-driven table.

rules.name: selective-checks-doc-sync
rules.evidence: Keep selective-checks behaviour and its documentation in sync... update `dev/breeze/doc/ci/04_selective_checks.md` in the same PR... and add/adjust tests in `dev/breeze/tests/test_selective_checks.py`.

rules.name: no-newsfragments-providers-ctl
rules.evidence: Never add newsfragments for `providers/` or `airflow-ctl/` — those distributions are released from `main` and their release managers regenerate the changelog from `git log`.

rules.name: newsfragment-user-facing-confirm
rules.evidence: Golden rule: never create a newsfragment unless you are certain the change is user-facing... Default to omitting it; a maintainer will ask for a newsfragment during review if the change warrants one.

rules.name: tests-use-pytest-not-unittest
rules.evidence: Use pytest patterns, not `unittest.TestCase`.

rules.name: tests-use-pytest-not-unittest
rules.evidence: Use pytest patterns, not `unittest.TestCase`.

rules.name: mock-patch-prefer-decorator
rules.evidence: Prefer `@mock.patch` decorators over `with mock.patch(...)` context managers for patching.

rules.name: mock-patch-prefer-decorator
rules.evidence: Prefer `@mock.patch` decorators over `with mock.patch(...)` context managers for patching.

rules.name: tests-no-datetime-now
rules.evidence: Use `time_machine` for time-dependent tests. Do not use `datetime.now()`

rules.name: tests-no-datetime-now
rules.evidence: Use `time_machine` for time-dependent tests. Do not use `datetime.now()`

rules.name: no-raw-log-text-assertions
rules.evidence: Do not assert on raw log text (`caplog.text` for example), these are legacy string matching APIs planned for removal.

rules.name: no-raw-log-text-assertions
rules.evidence: Do not assert on raw log text (`caplog.text` for example), these are legacy string matching APIs planned for removal.

rules.name: commit-message-conventions
rules.evidence: Write commit messages focused on user impact, not implementation details... Use the imperative mood and a plain message... The commit message body should describe why the change is made... and never what the change is.

rules.name: commit-no-conventional-prefix
rules.evidence: Bad: `fix(cli): dags test failure` — Airflow does not use Conventional Commits (`feat:`, `fix:`, `chore:` …). A `commit-msg` prek hook (`check-no-conventional-commit-message`) rejects these, and CI checks every commit of the PR.

rules.name: prek-install-before-commit
rules.evidence: Always run `prek install` before committing any code. It installs the `commit-msg` hook (in addition to `pre-commit`) so the Conventional Commits guard runs locally.

rules.name: verify-git-remote-naming
rules.evidence: Before running any remote-based command, run `git remote -v` and verify the names match this convention.

rules.name: no-push-to-upstream-or-main
rules.evidence: Always push branches to `origin`. Never push directly to `upstream` (and never push directly to `main` on either remote).

rules.name: check-existing-pr-before-new-pr
rules.evidence: Before working on an issue, check for open PRs already addressing it (`gh pr list --search "<issue number or keywords>"`...). Do not blindly open another near-identical PR for an issue that already has one.

rules.name: pre-push-self-review-checklist
rules.evidence: Before pushing, perform a self-review of your changes... Run regular (fast) static checks... Run manual (slower) checks... Before pushing, always rebase your branch onto the latest target branch (usually `main`).

rules.name: pr-title-and-body-conventions
rules.evidence: Review the PR title — keep it short (under 70 chars), in the imperative mood, and focused on user impact... Add a brief description of the changes at the top of the body. Reference related issues when applicable.

rules.name: pr-title-no-conventional-prefix
rules.evidence: Do not use Conventional Commits prefixes (`fix:`, `feat:`, `chore:`, …). apache/airflow does not follow that convention... The same rule applies to PR titles.

rules.name: avoid-issue-when-fix-imminent
rules.evidence: If you already know how to fix the problem and you (or the user) are going to open the PR shortly, do not file a GitHub issue first. Go straight to the PR.

rules.name: tracking-issue-for-workarounds
rules.evidence: When a PR applies a workaround, version cap, mitigation, or partial fix... the deferred work must be captured in a GitHub tracking issue... Open the tracking issue first, before finalising the PR body.

rules.name: github-post-attribution-footer
rules.evidence: Anything an agent drafts that ends up posted to GitHub on the user's account — PR / issue comments, PR-level reviews, line-level review comments, discussion replies — must end with an attribution footer.

rules.name: no-tagging-individuals-on-github
rules.evidence: AI agents MUST NOT mention or tag individual contributors, committers, PMC members, or maintainers using GitHub usernames (e.g. `@user`) unless explicitly instructed by a human reviewer.

rules.name: no-edit-apache-magpie-snapshot
rules.evidence: Adopter-specific modifications to framework-skill workflows live in `.apache-magpie-overrides/` — never edit the snapshot directly.

rules.name: no-hand-edit-uv-lock
rules.evidence: Never: ... Edit generated files by hand when a generation workflow exists. / The `uv.lock` file is generated by `uv lock`, `uv sync`... If at any point in time you have a conflict with `uv.lock`, simply delete it and run `uv lock` to regenerate it.

rules.name: no-destructive-git-operations
rules.evidence: Never: ... Use destructive git operations unless explicitly requested.

skill_candidates.summary: One-time environment setup: install prek, enable commit hooks, install the per-machine breeze shim.
skill_candidates.evidence:
Install prek: `uv tool install prek`
- Enable commit hooks: `prek install`
- Install breeze shim (one-time, per machine): `scripts/tools/setup_breeze` — installs `~/.local/bin/breeze` that runs breeze via `uvx` from the current git worktree's `dev/breeze`...

always_on: Environment: SQLite is the default backend; use --backend postgres/mysql for integration tests needing those databases; `docker network prune` fixes Docker networking failures.
Repository structure overview: UV workspace monorepo (airflow-core, task-sdk, providers, airflow-ctl, chart, dev, scripts).
Place temporary scripts in `dev/` (mounted as `/opt/airflow/dev/` inside Breeze).
Architecture boundaries: Scheduler never runs user code; Workers never access the metadata DB directly; API Server is the sole path to client-database interactions.
Security review guidance: distinguish actual vulnerabilities, known limitations, and deployment-hardening opportunities per `security_model.rst`.
Shared libraries live under `shared/` as separate small Python distributions, symlinked into consuming distributions.
Comment sparingly — code says what, comments say why; avoid narrating or repeated-rationale comments.
Imports at top of file, with exceptions for circular imports, lazy loading for worker isolation, and TYPE_CHECKING blocks.
Guard heavy type-only imports (e.g. `kubernetes.client`) with `TYPE_CHECKING` in multi-process code paths.
Name functions/methods with action verbs (`get_`, `extract_`, `find_`, `compute_`, `build_`); avoid noun-only names except predicates (`is_`, `has_`).
Apache License header required on all new files (already enforced by a prek hook).
Use `spec`/`autospec` when mocking; use `conf_vars` for Airflow config overrides (decorator for fixed values, context manager when parametrized).
Use `@pytest.mark.parametrize` to consolidate similar-input tests; use `@pytest.mark.db_test` for tests needing database access.
Test fixtures live in `devel-common/src/tests_common/pytest_plugin.py`; test location mirrors source layout.
Target exactly 100% coverage of what a PR changes — no tests for pre-existing logic or third-party/stdlib code, except deliberate integration tests.
Put generated output files (PR reviews, reports, scratch output) under `files/`, creating it if absent.
Prefer reviewing/building on an existing PR over opening a near-identical one — 'better PR wins' is not the default.
Ask first before large cross-package refactors, new dependencies with broad impact, or destructive data/migration changes; never commit secrets, credentials, or tokens.
Workaround/mitigation PRs must reference their tracking issue by number in the PR body and add its full URL as a comment at the workaround site in code, never vague forward-looking language.
Use the apache-magpie `magpie-pr-management-code-review` skill for PR review (preferred over an ad-hoc pass); run `/magpie-setup` on a fresh clone to populate the framework snapshot.

low_confidence.reason: Doc says 'in all prose' with no file-type restriction; path scoped to md/rst is inferred, and the regex cannot distinguish prose from legitimate code-token occurrences the doc says to preserve.
Same path-scoping inference as dag-title-case-prose, plus the rule has a 'except for historical context' carve-out a regex can't judge.

'Production code' vs test-file boundary is inferred (regex `^(?!.*/tests?/)`), not stated by the doc; pytest itself relies on assert in test files, so an imprecise exclusion risks blocking normal test writing.
The ban is scoped to duration measurement specifically; the regex matches any `time.time()` call, including legitimate non-duration uses.
The rule is scoped to the scheduler loop / synchronous interval tasks; the regex matches any `.query().delete()/.update()` anywhere in the codebase and can't verify absence of LIMIT/batching.
'Use pytest patterns, not unittest.TestCase' has no explicit never/do-not wording — reads as a standard-setting preference, arguable between require-read and a harder block.
Rule text scopes the ban to 'time-dependent tests', but the regex/path can't distinguish test code from production code where this specific rule doesn't apply.
Single regex spanning a potentially multi-line heredoc --body argument is fragile — risk of missing real @mentions in complex quoting, or false-positiving on email-like strings.
The document never states the literal CLI command for filing a GitHub issue; binding to `gh issue create` is inferred from convention, not the text.
Same as avoid-issue-when-fix-imminent — `gh issue create` is not literally named by the document.

Derived by combining the generic Boundaries prohibition ('never edit generated files by hand') with a separate uv.lock passage that itself only describes regeneration, not an explicit local ban on hand-editing.

The document names no specific destructive git commands ('destructive git operations' is generic); the regex's command list (reset --hard, clean -f, push --force, branch -D) is inferred from general git knowledge, not the text.

questions: 'No assert in production code' (block) — should it be scoped to exclude test files (e.g. paths matching `tests?/`), since pytest itself relies on `assert` there and a blanket block would halt normal test writing?

The bulk DELETE/UPDATE batching rule (block) — should it be scoped to scheduler/jobs modules only, or apply to any Python file calling `.query().delete()/.update()`, given the doc frames it as specific to 'the scheduler loop or synchronous interval tasks'?

The document never names a literal CLI command for filing a GitHub issue — should the two issue-related rules (avoid filing when a fix is imminent; open a tracking issue for workarounds) bind to `gh issue create`, or be dropped as unconfirmed triggers?
